content.whyBenefit: After this module you will understand how to design AI agents – from the cycle and tools to integrations and prompt architecture.
content.heroStat: You already know the 6 blocks and context engineering.
content.heroSubText: Design agents for your processes – depth levels and roles, multi-agent coordination, no-code tools. Context – Module 4.

slides.title: Agent engineering path – what you will find
slides.subtitle: Short overview; detail on later slides

sections.heading: In short
sections.body:
In this module, in order: **agent cycle** (diagram), **workflow concepts** (trigger, condition, action, webhook and more – full table and examples on another slide), **3A strategy**, **AI agent types and roles**, **5 workflow patterns**, **role and system prompt**, **AI tool selection**, **business automation platforms** (Zapier, Make, n8n, Power Automate), **hosting** (Railway-style PaaS – where an agent / API runs), **when to choose an agent**, **errors and limits**. Optional: **GitHub as code source**; spec and testing; glossary; summary.

We **do not repeat** term definitions here – find them on the **Key concepts** slide.

sections.heading: What you will find (order)
sections.body: **Agent cycle** → **3A strategy** → **AI agent types and roles** → **5 workflow patterns** → **Role and system prompt** → **Tool selection** → **When to use an agent** → **Errors and limits** → **Workflow concepts** → **Business automation tools** → **Hosting (PaaS)** → optional: **GitHub as code source**; spec/tests; **Glossary**; **summary**.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: On the next slide open **Agent cycle and architecture** – process diagram and cycle explanation there.
sections.copyable: Role: agent assistant. Task: review the process, use tools only when needed, return a clear result and say what failed if data is missing.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: If the answer has no multiple steps or tools, it is still a simple prompt. On the next slide you will see the full agent cycle.

slides.title: Agent cycle and architecture
slides.subtitle: Agent → Planning → Tools → Environment → Result → Feedback

sections.heading: How an AI agent works
sections.body: The agent plans actions, uses tools, reads the environment and evaluates the result. View full size – the same image opens in a modal.

sections.heading: How an agent works
sections.body:
An AI agent runs in **five steps**: **(1)** Agent receives a task, **(2)** Planning – break into steps, **(3)** Tools (search, API, files – if the platform allows), **(4)** Environment – context and response, **(5)** Result with feedback back to the agent.

**Cycle in practice:** understand → choose tool → execute → based on the result, decide whether to repeat the step or finish. Unlike a simple chat – the agent **autonomously** chooses actions and can call external tools.

sections.heading: When to use an agent (short)
sections.body:
- You need **several steps**, not one answer.
- Hard to describe everything with **pure rules**.
- There is **unstructured data** (text, emails, feedback) that must be interpreted.

A **simple prompt** is enough when one clear question and one answer – no search or external systems.

**More detail** – with examples and an agent template – on **When to choose an agent vs a simple prompt**.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Copy the task below into AI and run it.

sections.heading: Copyable AI task
sections.body: Task for AI (paste into chat): “Explain in your own words **in five steps** (agent, planning, tools, environment, result) how an AI agent works, and **in one sentence** – how it differs from one question and one answer without tools.”
sections.copyable: Explain in your own words in five steps (agent, planning, tools, environment, result) how an AI agent works, and in one sentence – how it differs from one question and one answer without tools.

slides.title: Checkpoint: agent cycle
slides.subtitle: Agent engineering path – step 1

content.title: Agent cycle – I ran 1 agent request
content.body: Run a short agent request with search or a tool. Mark this step when the answer shows not only text, but clear steps, sources, or a tool trail.

sections.heading: What to check
sections.body: Does the answer have at least 2 steps? Does it say which tool the AI used — or why it did not?
sections.copyable: Task: [DESCRIBE]. Use search or a tool if needed. Return the answer in 3 steps: what you did, which tool you used, what the result was. If you did not use a tool – explain why.

sections.heading: If it failed
sections.body: Add sentences to the task: “use search”, “provide sources”, “if not found – write Not found”.

slides.title: Quick check: agent cycle
slides.subtitle: 3 questions about agents, tools and failure messages

questions.question: Which sign best shows that this is an agent-style task?
options: Several steps, tool use or a decision whether to repeat an action
One simple question and one answer
Only a nicer answer tone
Only a longer prompt without tools
questions.explanation: An agent-style task has multi-step logic, tool use or a decision whether to repeat an action. Without that, a simple prompt is often enough.

questions.question: When is a simple prompt enough instead of an agent?
options: When you need one clear answer without search, files or several steps
When you need search with sources
When you need several roles and handoff rules
When you need a workflow with a trigger and actions
questions.explanation: A simple prompt fits one clear question. Choose an agent when you need tools, several steps or decisions between steps.

questions.question: What should an agent return if it cannot use a tool?
options: A short reason and a suggestion for what to fix
An empty answer
Invented data
Only a generic motivational phrase
questions.explanation: A safe agent explains why it failed and says what to fix. This is part of error handling.

slides.title: 3A strategy
slides.subtitle: Automatize 80 % / Augment 15 % / Autonomize 5 %

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: 3A is a decision portfolio: most work uses rules (**fewer errors**), a smaller share is human + AI, and the smallest band is agents with limits.

sections.heading: 3A strategy (diagram)
sections.body: Tap a band – when to choose it. Then assign your own processes below.

sections.heading: Example
sections.body: **Invoices / form → CRM → confirmation email** → **Automatize**, because the rule is clear and no human decision is needed.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: List **three work processes** and assign each to a 3A band. Copy the template into AI or write it down.

sections.heading: Copyable template
sections.body: Fill [PROCESS] and choose a band for each row.
sections.copyable:
Process 1: [PROCESS]. 3A band: Automatize / Augment / Autonomize. Why (1 sentence): [REASON]. Human approval: [YES/NO + when].

Process 2: [PROCESS]. 3A band: … Why: … Human approval: …

Process 3: [PROCESS]. 3A band: … Why: … Human approval: …

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Can you explain why at least one process is **not** Autonomize (5%) if you chose Automatize or Augment? Can you name a process that **requires** a human? If not – return to the diagram or the example.

sections.heading: Where to apply
sections.body: When planning business automation: which processes need only rules (80), which need human approval (15), where limited agent autonomy is OK (5).

content.footer: Next – slide 7: When does a human approve?

slides.title: When does a human approve?
slides.subtitle: Control mode by consequence and reversibility

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: Human oversight (**Human-in-the-loop / HITL**) is not a maturity ladder to autopilot. It is a **risk architecture**: the higher the consequence and the harder it is to reverse a mistake, the earlier a human must step in.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Pick a scenario and a control mode. The simulator shows whether your choice fits, what happens on an error case, and generates a rule for the 10.64 brief.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Can you name at least one process where **outcome monitoring alone is not enough**? Will your 10.64 human-control line include mode, condition, escalation and an audit record?

content.footer: Next – slide 8: Role and system prompt template

slides.title: Role and system prompt template
slides.subtitle: How to set role and limits for an agent

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: Role defines how AI behaves – in simple chat and in agent systems. System prompt – where role, limits and principles are set. This slide is a simple system prompt; multi-agent roles come on slide 10.45.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Copy the prompt below into your AI tool’s “system settings” field (if available). Then ask one agent-style query.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt
sections.body: Below – system prompt you can copy into AI tool settings.
sections.copyable:
Your role – assistant that completes tasks step by step.
Use tools (search, calculators, etc.) when they help the answer.
If the task is unclear or fails – briefly say why and what to fix.
Do not invent data – if facts are needed, use search or write “I don’t know”.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: If AI does not use tools when needed – check whether the platform has agent features enabled (Browse, Tools). If the answer has no sources – ask “provide sources” or specify sources in the input.

slides.title: AI agent types and roles
slides.subtitle: Depth levels L0–L3 and multi-agent roles for business

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: Not every task needs an AI team. First set the **depth level** (L0–L3), then whether you need several **roles**. Below – the diagram; start at L0.

sections.heading: Depth and team roles
sections.body: Left – depth; right – L2 team example (appears when you select L2).

sections.heading: 3A tip
sections.body: 3A reminder: **Automatize** ≈ L3 flow; **Augment** ≈ L1; **Autonomize** ≈ L2 + evaluator. Details – earlier 3A slide.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Pick one work process. First write **L0 / L1 / L2 / L3**. If L2 – define three roles (coordinator, specialist, evaluator). **Router** – only if you need triage before the coordinator.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt
sections.body: Copy and fill [X] with your process.
sections.copyable:
Process: [X]
Depth level (L0–L3): [L?]
If L2 – three roles:
1) Coordinator – what they plan and delegate (+ input / output)
2) Specialist – what they do specifically (+ input / output)
3) Evaluator – what they check before delivery (+ input / output)
One sentence per role.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Did you choose a **depth level**? If L2 – does each role have a clear **input** and **output**? If not – start with L1 (one agent).

sections.heading: When NOT to use multi-agent
sections.body: One agent is enough for a simple task. Multi-agent too early if there is no HITL or clear “done” criterion. Start simple – add roles only when one agent is no longer enough.

content.footer: Next – slide 8: Checkpoint: roles and handoff

slides.title: Checkpoint: roles and handoff
slides.subtitle: Agent engineering path – step 2

content.title: Roles – I defined inputs and outputs
content.body: You already chose the level on slide 10.45. Here – only **input, output, and handoff rule** for three roles (if L2). If you stayed on L1 – mark the step and continue.

sections.heading: What to check
sections.body: Is it clear when the specialist hands work to the evaluator? Does the evaluator have a done criterion?
sections.copyable:
Process: [DESCRIBE] (level already: L2).
For each role (coordinator / specialist / evaluator):
– input
– output
– handoff rule: when work passes to the next role.

sections.heading: If too complex
sections.body: Reduce to L1: one agent, one tool, one result.

slides.title: 5 workflow patterns for business
slides.subtitle: Chain, routing, parallel, coordinator, evaluator

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: When one AI agent is no longer enough, use **workflow patterns** – not programming, but a clear plan of roles and handoffs.

sections.heading: 5 patterns
sections.body:
**1. Chain** – one step after another (query → classification → draft → send).

**2. Routing** – different branch by type (complaint / inquiry / proposal).

**3. Parallel work** – several specialists at once, then merge (CRM + email → one summary).

**4. Coordinator + specialists** – dynamically assigns sub-tasks (weekly report: data + trends → 1 page).

**5. Generator + evaluator** – draft → QC → fix (email, report, FAQ).

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Pick one pattern for your process and note which roles perform each step.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt (coordinator)
sections.body: Copy and fill [DESCRIBE].
sections.copyable:
You are the coordinator. Task: [DESCRIBE].
Break into 2–3 sub-tasks. For each assign a role (specialist / evaluator),
input, output and handoff rule (when to pass to the next role).
Present the plan as a numbered list.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Does the plan have a clear **completion criterion**? Do risky steps have **HITL** (human approval)?

sections.heading: When NOT to use multi-agent
sections.body: If the task has 1–2 steps – L1 (one agent) is enough. Multi-agent without an evaluator is risky for finance, support and public content.

slides.title: Agent orchestration walkthrough
slides.subtitle: Router ≠ orchestrator; validation ≠ evaluation; targeted retry

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: The orchestrator does not do specialist work itself – it manages the plan, state and fix cycles. Three distinctions: **Router = WHERE**, **orchestrator = HOW**; **validation** checks format/sources, the **evaluator** decides Approve / Retry / Human; on error – a **targeted** retry, not a full rerun.

sections.heading: Walkthrough
sections.body: Scenario: EU AI Act – hiring AI checklist. Step through the 6 macro steps on the diagram.
sections.imageAlt: Agent orchestration flow: input, router, orchestrator, specialists, validation, evaluator, output

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Go through all 6 steps. At step 5 notice the **RETRY** loop – what returns to the orchestrator and why the whole run is not restarted.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Can you state in one sentence how a **router** differs from an **orchestrator**? Do you know when the evaluator says **Retry** versus **hand off to a human**?

sections.heading: Three distinctions (deeper)
sections.body:
**1. Router vs orchestrator** – the router classifies the request before execution; the orchestrator manages the active plan, state and retries.

**2. Agents vs tools** – agents have a role and rules; tools (search, APIs) are an external layer agents call.

**3. Validation vs evaluation** – validation checks format and sources; the evaluator makes the system decision (Approve / Retry / Human).

Different from slide **120.5**: here – architecture walkthrough; there – a simple business multi-agent schema without coding.

slides.title: Warm-up: workflow and orchestration
slides.subtitle: Questions on templates and orchestration

questions.question: Which workflow pattern fits when one step must happen after another?
options: Chain
Routing
Parallel work
Only one question without a pattern
questions.explanation: A chain fits when one step clearly follows another: query → classification → draft → send.

questions.question: Which pattern fits when a request is routed by type?
options: Routing
Chain
Generator + evaluator
Only the 3A strategy
questions.explanation: Routing sends a request by type or category: complaint, inquiry, proposal and similar branches.

questions.question: How does a router differ from an orchestrator in an agent system?
options: Router = where to go; orchestrator = how to manage plan, state and retry
They do the same thing – only names differ
Orchestrator only classifies type; router manages specialists
Router always restarts the whole flow after an error
questions.explanation: A router directs the request by type (WHERE). An orchestrator manages the active plan, state and targeted retry (HOW) – not a full restart.

slides.title: Closed learning loop
slides.subtitle: Agent engineering

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: An agent does more than run one task. A good system keeps an action history, checks the result, extracts lessons from failures, and updates rules or skills for the next run.

sections.heading: Two system layers
sections.body:
**Execution system:** task → rules → coordinator → specialists / evaluator → skills → result.

**Learning loop:** action history → check → lessons → update. Failures from one attempt become a better next attempt.

sections.heading: Blocks in plain language
sections.body:
**Rules** – limits and principles: what AI must not do, when to ask a human.

**Skills** – reusable abilities: search, writing, checking, or one Skill pack with instructions.

**Action history** – task, steps, errors, human notes.

**Check** – test or evaluation: did the result meet the criteria.

**Lessons** – a short note: what failed, what to change, where to update a rule or skill.

**Update** – changed rule, prompt, skill description, or test for next time.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: After one small scenario, write one lesson: what failed, which rule or skill fixes it, and where that fix will be used next time.

sections.heading: Copyable lesson template
sections.body: Use after an M12 scenario or any agent attempt.
sections.copyable:
Lesson after an agent attempt:
1) What happened?
2) What was the cause?
3) What do I update: rule, prompt, skill, or test?
4) How will I check next time?

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: A good lesson changes the next attempt. If you only describe a fact (“it failed”) but do not update a rule, skill, or test – the learning loop does not close.

slides.title: Tool selection and limits
slides.subtitle: What AI can use – search, files, API; platforms (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini)

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: Not all AI tools have the same tools. In the task clearly write: “Use search and provide sources”. Tell the user (or yourself) what is available on the chosen platform.

sections.heading: AI platforms – what to check
sections.body: Before writing an agent prompt, check your AI tool: is **search**, **files**, **API / Tools** enabled? **ChatGPT**, **Claude** and **Gemini** differ – state clearly what to use (e.g. “Use search and cite sources”).

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Open your AI tool settings and note which tools you allow (search, files, API). Then in one task clearly write: “Use search and provide sources”.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt
sections.body: Below – prompt with search instruction; copy and fill [DESCRIBE TASK].
sections.copyable:
Task: [DESCRIBE TASK].
Use search to find the latest information. If you find sources – list them at the end of the answer. If not – write “Not found” and briefly explain what you searched for.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: If the answer has no links – did you say “use search”? Is the tool enabled on the platform?

sections.heading: Tool selection tree (interactive diagram)
sections.body: Pick a branch in the diagram – confirmation at the bottom. Full description – in **Business automation tools**, §21.

sections.heading: Tool selection tree (for workflow tools)
sections.body: Briefly: Office 365 → **Power Automate**; fast start → **Zapier**; complex logic → **Make**; self-host → **n8n**. Full comparison and “pick your tool” task – slide **Business automation tools**. More in the automation tools doc, §21.

slides.title: When to choose an agent vs a simple prompt
slides.subtitle: Complex tasks with tools – agent; one question – simple prompt

sections.heading: In short
sections.body:
Agent – when the task is complex (several actions, external data, tools). Simple prompt – when one question, one answer, no external tools.

**Multi-agent patterns** – slide **10.48**. Full 5-part template and workflow spec – expandable below; checkpoint – **10.51**.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Copy the example, fill [ROLE] and [X], run AI. Does the answer include error handling and sources?
sections.copyable: Role: [ROLE]. Task: (1) Search for [X], (2) pick 3–5 key sources, (3) write a short summary with sources. If not found – write “Not found” and why.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt (example)
sections.body: Short agent-task example – fill [ROLE] and [X].
sections.copyable: Role: [ROLE]. Task: (1) Search [X], (2) pick 3–5 key sources, (3) write a summary in English with links. If not found – write “Not found” and why.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Does your agent prompt include **error handling** and a **tool instruction**? If missing – add them before checkpoint **10.51**.
sections.copyable:
ROLE
You are [domain / role]. Your responsibility is [what you do]. You have access to [search / calculator / API].
TASK
[Describe the task in steps.]
TOOLS
Use [tool] when [condition].
OUTPUT
[Format.]
ERRORS
If you cannot complete the task: write “Failed: [reason]” and suggest a fix.

sections.heading: Optional: 5-part template, rules, workflow spec
sections.body:
**5 parts:** (1) Role, (2) Task in steps, (3) Tools, (4) Output format, (5) Error handling.

**Full template** and workflow one-pager live here when you need them. More testing detail – slide **10.65**.
sections.copyable:
ROLE
You are [domain / role]. Responsibility – [what you do]. Access to [search / API]. Do not: [limits].

TASK (steps)
(1) [First]
(2) [Second]
(3) [Third]

TOOLS
Use [search] when [when]. Cite sources at the end.

FORMAT
[Table / list]. Language: English.

ERROR HANDLING
“Failed: [reason]” + what to fix.

sections.heading: Workflow specification (1 page)
sections.body: When the agent connects to a form, CRM, email or another tool, write a one-page workflow spec before go-live: trigger, input schema, actions, output, error handling, and human approval where needed.
sections.copyable:
Workflow specification:
1) Trigger – what starts the flow.
2) Input schema – which fields are required.
3) Actions – what the system does.
4) Output – what the user receives.
5) Error handling – what happens on failure.
6) Human approval – when a person must confirm.

slides.title: Checkpoint: agent prompt
slides.subtitle: Agent engineering path – step 3

content.title: Agent prompt – I have a template before practice
content.body: Before Module 12 practice, have one 5-part agent prompt, a workflow draft, and 1 golden check (edge case). The **full minimum brief** (spec + 3 tests + human approval) is slide **10.64** after the tools section. This is your start for the Coordinator + 2 specialists scenario.

sections.heading: Minimum artefact
sections.body: Role, task in steps, tools, output format, error handling + Trigger, actions, output, human approval. Finish the full ticket (3 test cases + HITL) on slide **10.64**.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: If error handling or human approval is missing in a risky place, add it before you continue.

sections.heading: Golden check
sections.body: Test the prompt with 1 edge case: a required field is missing, no source exists, or the API does not respond. A good result – AI returns a clear “Failed: [reason]” and suggests what to fix.
sections.copyable: Check this agent prompt with an edge case: [PASTE PROMPT]. Test situation: [missing required field / no source / API timeout]. Judge whether the answer has “Failed: [reason]” and a clear fix suggestion.

slides.title: Error handling and limits
slides.subtitle: What to do when something fails

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: The agent must know what to do when something fails. Set limits: what not to do, what to return instead of an empty answer.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Paste the text below into **system** or **user** prompt (or both). Then ask AI a deliberately impossible task – e.g. “Search for the 2029 profit report of non-existent company X and provide real sources”.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt
sections.body: Add to system or user prompt – what to do when it fails.
sections.copyable: If the task cannot be completed (missing data, disallowed action or timeout): write a short message “Failed: [reason]” and suggest what the user can fix. Never return an empty answer.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Did AI return **“Failed”** (or similar) and **reason**, not an empty answer? If it stays silent – add to prompt: “If you cannot – always say why.”

slides.title: Warm-up: before workflow terms
slides.subtitle: 3 questions: error handling, agent vs prompt, search

questions.question: What is a good result when AI cannot complete the task?
options: A clear “Failed: [reason]” plus what to fix
An empty answer
Invented facts so the answer looks complete
Only an apology with no reason
questions.explanation: Error handling needs a clear failure message and a next step — not an empty or invented answer.

questions.question: When is a simple prompt usually enough?
options: One question, one answer, no external tools
You need search with sources and several steps
You need several roles and handoff rules
You need a form → CRM → email flow
questions.explanation: Without tools and multi-step logic, a simple prompt is enough. Otherwise use an agent template.

questions.question: What should you state clearly if you need web facts?
options: “Use search and provide sources”
“Answer as long as possible”
“Do not use any tools”
“Guess if you do not know”
questions.explanation: Without an explicit tool instruction, AI may answer without sources. “Use search…” reduces that risk.

content.footer: Next – Core concepts: trigger, action, condition, webhook

slides.title: Key concepts: trigger, action, condition, webhook
slides.subtitle: Workflow structure and examples

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: Workflow sequence: **Trigger → Condition → Action**. **Webhook** – an event from another system that starts the flow. These 4 concepts are enough to describe your process.

sections.heading: Workflow chain (diagram)
sections.body: Core sequence: **Trigger → Condition → Action**. A **Webhook** often starts the same kind of flow as a trigger (e.g. payment). **View full size** – same image in a modal.

sections.heading: Four core concepts
sections.body:
**Trigger** – the event that starts a workflow (new email, form submit, file in a folder).

**Condition** – a rule for the next step (e.g. if value > 500 – send to manager).

**Action** – a system action (email, CRM record, Slack message).

**Webhook** – real-time data handoff: event → call to another system address (e.g. payment → order update).

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Write down **one real trigger** from your work and **two actions** you want automated. Copy the template and fill it – including an error step.

sections.heading: Copyable template
sections.body: Fill the [ ] fields – your first draft workflow schema.
sections.copyable: Trigger: [EVENT]. Condition (if needed): [RULE]. Action 1: [ACTION]. Action 2: [ACTION]. Webhook? [YES/NO – from where]. Error step: [what to do if it fails].

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Is your trigger an **event** (not a task), and action a **system action**? Did you name an error step? If mixed up – return to “Four core concepts”.

sections.heading: Extra concepts (if needed)
sections.body:
**Integration** – how systems connect (Zapier/Make, API, file exchange).

**API** – agreed way for programs to exchange data; AI agents often call APIs as tools.

**Polling** – periodic “any new data?” (unlike webhook).

**Error handling** – what to do when a step fails: retry, alert a human, stop.

**Logs** – what ran, when, success or failure.

**Example:** form (trigger) → email check (condition) → CRM + email + Slack (actions).

slides.title: Business automation tools
slides.subtitle: Zapier, Make, n8n, Power Automate – when to choose what

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: Four common tools: **Zapier** – fast start; **Make** – more complex logic; **n8n** – control on your servers; **Power Automate** – Microsoft ecosystem. Pick one for your context. They automate **processes** – if you need a 24/7 app, API or AI agent hosted online, see the next slide.

sections.heading: When to pick which
sections.body:
**Non-tech team, fast start** → **Zapier** (e.g. Calendly → calendar → reminder email).

**Need conditions, loops, better pricing** → **Make.com** (e.g. Shopify order → inventory → supplier alert).

**Tech team, data on your side** → **n8n** (e.g. payment webhook → DB → Slack alert).

**Daily Teams / Outlook / SharePoint** → **Power Automate** (e.g. Outlook PDF → SharePoint → Teams).

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Pick **one** tool for your process. Copy the template and fill it – a decision draft before Module 12 practice.

sections.heading: Copyable template
sections.body: Fill [PROCESS] and the chosen tool.
sections.copyable: Process: [PROCESS]. Chosen tool: Zapier / Make / n8n / Power Automate. Why (1 sentence): [REASON]. Trigger → Action (brief): [SCHEMA].

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Can you explain why you did **not** pick another tool? If not – return to “When to pick which”.

sections.heading: More detail on each
sections.body:
**Zapier:** 7000+ integrations; fast start; weakness – cost at scale.

**Make.com:** drag & drop, conditions and loops; weakness – learning curve.

**n8n:** open-source, self-host; weakness – needs IT skills.

**Power Automate:** Microsoft security and licensing; weakness – limited outside Microsoft.

More in **Business automation tools** doc. Interactive tree – slide **Tool selection and limits**.

slides.title: Hosting: where the agent and API run
slides.subtitle: Workflow vs RPA vs AI agent vs Railway-style PaaS

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: **Four layers:** workflow (Zapier / Make / n8n) arranges steps; RPA (UiPath) clicks UI when there is no API; an AI agent analyses and decides; **hosting (PaaS)** is the place online where an app, API or agent runs 24/7 (Railway, Render, Fly.io). One idea: workflow arranges; PaaS runs. If you write code, the source is often **GitHub** – more on the optional slide next.

sections.heading: When to pick – run modes
sections.body: Pick a mode based on whether the app must listen for events or a schedule is enough.

sections.heading: When to pick – PaaS orientation
sections.body: Three common hosting choices (no prices – they change often).

rows: API + database + agent for a fast start
When you need to run close to users in different regions

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Does your scenario need **hosting** (app / API runs continuously)? Yes or no. If yes – pick a mode (always-on / cron / webhook+worker) and fill the template.

sections.heading: Copyable template
sections.body: Fill the [ ] fields.
sections.copyable:
Process: [PROCESS]
Workflow: Zapier / Make / n8n / Power Automate / none
AI agent: yes / no
RPA (UI, no API): yes / no
Code: GitHub repo / local only / none
Hosting: Railway / Render / Fly.io / not needed
Mode: always-on / cron / webhook+worker
Why (1 sentence): [ ]

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Can you tell **in one sentence** how n8n differs from Railway? (n8n arranges the flow; Railway runs the app.) If not – return to “In short”.

sections.heading: More: hybrid and pitfalls
sections.body:
**Hybrid:** form → n8n → AI agent → Postgres → email; agent, API and DB run on PaaS (e.g. Railway).

**Analogy:** n8n = process lead · agent = worker · Postgres = archive · Railway = office.

**Avoid:** (1) an AI agent where Zapier is enough; (2) RPA when an API exists; (3) serverless-only for a Discord bot – you need always-on.

**Code and deploy:** if you write an app – see the optional slide **GitHub as code source**.

More in **Business automation tools** doc, section “Hosting (PaaS)”.

content.footer: Next – slide 25: GitHub as code source

slides.title: GitHub as code source
slides.subtitle: GitHub stores – PaaS runs

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: **GitHub** stores project code and versions (source of truth). **Deploy platforms** (Railway, Render, Vercel) pull the latest code and run it online. One idea: GitHub stores, PaaS runs. No-code (Zapier/Make) often does **not** need GitHub.

sections.heading: When to pick – platforms
sections.body: Different places do different jobs.

rows: Backend, APIs, bots, AI agents, databases

sections.heading: Flow
sections.body: Typical path (orientation): **local work** → save changes to GitHub (commit / push) → platform **auto-deploy** → site, API or agent runs on Railway / Render / Vercel. This is not a git tutorial – understanding the direction is enough.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Does your scenario need a **GitHub repo**? Yes or no – one sentence why. Fill the template.

sections.heading: Copyable template
sections.body: Fill the [ ] fields; paste the prompt into AI.
sections.copyable:
Process: [PROCESS]
Code on GitHub: yes / no / not yet
Hosting: Railway / Render / Vercel / not needed
Why (1 sentence): [ ]

---
AI prompt:
Explain the difference: GitHub vs Railway. My project: [DESCRIPTION].
Do I need GitHub + auto-deploy, or is no-code (Zapier/Make) enough? Answer in 5 lines.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Can you tell **“stores code”** (GitHub) from **“runs it”** (Railway / Render)? If not – return to “In short”.

sections.heading: More: typical path and pitfalls
sections.body:
**Commands in one line (if you already use git):** add → commit → push.

**Replit** – fast prototype or classroom demo; not the same as production Railway/Render.

**Avoid:** code only on your laptop (no repo) when you need reliable deploy or teamwork.

More in **Business automation tools** doc, §21b “GitHub as source”.

slides.title: Minimum workflow brief
slides.subtitle: 1-page schema + 3 tests + when a human approves

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: Before Module 12 practices, fill a **minimum workflow brief**: 1-page schema + 3 test cases + a human-control rule. Carry the same brief into 10.66 QC and the labs.

sections.heading: Minimum brief
sections.body:
**(A)** 1-page spec: trigger → error handling.
**(B)** 3 test cases: happy path, missing field, API/timeout or webhook duplicate.
**(C)** Human-control rule – mode, condition / limit, reject or escalate, audit record.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Copy the template and fill it for your process. Next – the 10.66 evaluator.

sections.heading: Copyable template
sections.body: Minimum workflow brief.
sections.copyable:
Minimum workflow brief:
1) Trigger – what starts the flow.
2) Input schema – which fields are required.
3) Condition – what branches the flow.
4) Actions – 3–5 steps with tools.
5) Output – what result must be created.
6) Error handling – what to do if a step fails.
7) Audit record – what to log: time, step, result.
8) Human-control rule – mode: [every case / exceptions / post-action / monitoring]; condition or limit: […]; reject / escalate: […]; audit record: […].

Test cases:
B1 happy path: …
B2 missing field: …
B3 timeout / duplicate: …

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Do you have **B** and **C** (not only happy path)? Does the human-control rule include mode, condition, escalation and an audit record? If finance / personal data is marked “never” – return to 10.26.

slides.title: Workflow testing and security
slides.subtitle: Specification, 10 test scenarios, security, tools tree

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: **Deep guide after the 10.64 brief.** If your minimum brief (spec + 3 tests + human approval) is already filled – this slide is **optional**: diagrams, extended tests, incident playbook, security. Deep QC – next slide **Agent QC evaluator**.

sections.heading: Workflow specification (diagram)
sections.body: **8 specification blocks** as one sequence: from trigger to audit log. **View full size** – same image in a modal.

sections.heading: 5 quick tests (before go-live)
sections.body:
(1) Empty required fields. (2) Invalid email format. (3) Duplicates – same record twice. (4) API timeout. (5) Auth error (401/403).

**Idempotency:** unique request ID – if the record exists, **update**, do not create new.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Pick **2 tests** from the list for your workflow draft. Then go to **Agent QC evaluator** – paste your spec into the evaluator prompt.

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Do you have at least **trigger + error handling + 1 test case**? If not – add them before the QC evaluator.

sections.heading: Incident playbook (diagram)
sections.body: **5 incident steps:** stop, record, assess scope, notify, fix. **View full size** – same image in a modal.

sections.heading: More detail (spec, 10 tests, security)
sections.body:
**8-block spec:** Trigger, input schema, Condition, Actions, Output, SLA/limits, error handling, audit log.

**Extra tests:** webhook duplicates, special chars, invalid values, missing fields, rate limit (429).

**3 AI quality tests:** empty input → “Failed”; false fact – does not confirm; long text – does not crash silently.

**Security:** mask personal data, API keys not in code, human approval at risky points.

**Tool tree:** see slide **Tool selection and limits**. Full guide – automation tools doc (§18–21).

sections.heading: Tool selection tree
sections.body: We **do not repeat** the table here – the same tree and interactive diagram are on the **Tool selection and limits** slide. See that slide for the interactive schema; more detail in the automation tools doc.

sections.heading: Full guide
sections.body: Full material – **Business automation tools** doc: Zapier / Make / n8n / Power Automate choice, testing, and security.

slides.title: Agent QC evaluator
slides.subtitle: One prompt checks an agent or workflow specification

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: One evaluator prompt checks your **10.64 minimum brief** (or 10.51 draft): agent prompt / workflow specification. The goal is to find unclear points before practice.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Copy the evaluator, paste your **10.64 brief** Copy result (or 10.51 draft), and fix at least one point that AI marks as “fix”.

sections.heading: Copyable prompt (evaluator)
sections.body: One prompt evaluates another: the evaluator does not create a new workflow, it shows what to fix.
sections.copyable:
You are an agent quality evaluator.
Evaluate the agent or workflow specification below against 5 criteria:
1) goal and completion criterion,
2) input fields,
3) tools and limits,
4) error handling,
5) human-control rule: mode, condition / limit, reject or escalate, audit record.

Return a table: criterion | OK / fix | what is unclear | concrete fix.
If data is missing, do not guess – say which field is needed.
Specification: [PASTE HERE]

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: A good evaluation has at least one concrete fix or a clear “OK” for every criterion. If you only get generic praise, ask for a table with concrete fixes.

practicalTask.title: Steps: evaluator checks your specification
practicalTask.template:
1. Copy the evaluator prompt from the block above.
2. Paste it into AI.
3. Paste your agent prompt or workflow specification.
4. Fix at least one point the evaluator marked as “fix”.
5. Run the evaluation again and check whether all 5 criteria have a clear answer.

The evaluator and your prompt are not competitors: the evaluator helps improve your artefact before practice.

slides.title: Glossary
slides.subtitle: Agent engineering terms – want more detail?

terms.definition: System that completes tasks in steps and can use tools (search, API, files).
Function AI can call (e.g. search, calculator, file read).
Setting “who you are” and “how to behave” – visible to AI, not the user.
Task entered by the user.
Connection between AI and external data or services (API, database).
Step completion – planning and tool calls.
Rules the agent does not break (e.g. disallowed actions, privacy).
Keeping a human in approval, exception review or monitoring for AI / agent actions. Choose the mode by consequence and reversibility (slide 10.26) – not as a maturity ladder to full autonomy.
What to return to the user when the task fails.
Event that starts a workflow (e.g. new email, form submission).
Action the system performs (e.g. send email, write to CRM).
Rule – when to run the next step (e.g. if value > €500).
Real-time data transfer between systems (event → API call).
Place online where an app, API or AI agent runs continuously (e.g. Railway, Render, Fly.io). Not the same as Zapier / n8n – those arrange the flow; PaaS runs it.
The app runs without stopping and listens for events (e.g. Telegram bot). Different from cron (on a schedule) or a one-shot webhook.
Place where project code and versions are stored. Deploy platforms (Railway, Render, Vercel) often connect to the repo and run the latest version.
Robotic process automation – a bot clicks UI when there is no API (e.g. UiPath). When an API exists, prefer workflow tools, not RPA.
Multi-agent role – breaks down task, delegates to specialists, merges results.
Multi-agent role – checks quality and rules; can return for revision (QC).
Multi-agent role – routes the request to the right role or flow by type.
Cycle: understand → choose tool → execute → observe result → repeat or finish.

slides.title: Warm-up before the test
slides.subtitle: 3 questions: agent vs prompt, 3A, orchestration

questions.question: When should you choose an agent instead of a simple prompt?
options: When you need several steps, tools, or a decide-whether-to-retry loop
When you need one short answer without search
When you only want a nicer tone
When the prompt is longer than 2 sentences
questions.explanation: An agent fits complex tasks with steps and tools. For one clear answer, a simple prompt is often enough.

questions.question: Which 3A band means “human decides, AI assists”?
options: Augment (15 %)
Automatize (80 %)
Autonomize (5 %)
L0 taxonomy only
questions.explanation: Augment keeps the human in the decision; AI drafts summaries or recommendations before approval.

questions.question: How does a router differ from an orchestrator?
options: Router = where to go; orchestrator = how to manage plan, state and retry
They do the same thing
The orchestrator only classifies type
The router always restarts the whole flow
questions.explanation: A router directs by type (WHERE). An orchestrator manages the active plan and targeted retry (HOW).

slides.title: Questions
slides.subtitle: Agent cycle, tools, prompts

testQuestions.scenarioContext: An AI agent gives a confident answer but does not cite sources, even though the task required up-to-date facts.
testQuestions.question: What should be fixed first?
options: Clearly state the tool and rule: use search, cite sources, and if none are found, write “Not found”
Increase answer length to 2000 words
Remove the error-handling sentence
Keep the same prompt and just ask again
testQuestions.explanation: The agent needs clear context: which tool to use, when to cite sources and what to return when sources are not found. This reduces invented-fact risk.

testQuestions.question: Where are AI role and limits set in an agent system?
options: Only in user prompt
In system prompt
Only in tool list
Nowhere
testQuestions.explanation: System prompt sets role, principles and limits – visible to AI, not the user.

testQuestions.question: How does an evaluator differ from a specialist in multi-agent?
options: Evaluator checks quality; specialist does the work
Evaluator always sends emails; specialist only reads
They do the same, just with different tools
Evaluator sets the trigger
testQuestions.explanation: Specialist does narrow work (search, draft). Evaluator checks rules, quality and can return for revision – separate QC role.

testQuestions.question: What to do when the agent cannot complete the task?
options: Nothing – wait
Specify in system or user prompt: write “Failed” and reason
Turn off tools
Repeat the same prompt
testQuestions.explanation: Error handling – clearly state what to return (e.g. “Failed: [reason]” and what to fix).

testQuestions.question: What is a router in an AI team?
options: Role that routes the request to the right role by type
Tool that sends emails automatically
Same as coordinator – synonym
AI model version
testQuestions.explanation: Router classifies input and routes to the right specialist or flow – like reception or triage.

testQuestions.scenarioContext: You receive an RFP document and want: researcher gathers facts, writer prepares summary, evaluator checks criteria.
testQuestions.question: Which workflow pattern fits best?
options: Coordinator + specialists (or chain with roles)
Only one prompt without role
Routing by email size
Parallel work without merging
testQuestions.explanation: RFP with several roles – typical L2 (AI team): coordinator plans, specialists execute, evaluator checks. Chain with roles also works.

testQuestions.question: Scenario: a client fills a form on the website; you want the new entry to go to Google Sheets and the client to get email confirmation. What should you do first when designing the flow (Zapier, Make, etc.)?
options: Define the trigger – what starts the flow (e.g. new form submission), then actions (Sheets, email)
First write only a long AI chat text with no link to form or email
Only pick Slack channel name and colour for the notification
Launch to production and then think about errors
testQuestions.explanation: First – event that starts the flow (trigger) and input schema; then actions (entry, email). Clear what starts the chain and what data moves.

testQuestions.scenarioContext: A workflow prepares a customer reply and sometimes sees personal data. Standard replies repeat, but a wrong reply to a customer would be hard to reverse.
testQuestions.question: Which human-control mode fits best?
options: Exception review: standard cases run automatically; risky or unclear cases go to a human
Outcome monitoring only: a human sees only a weekly dashboard
Post-action spot checks: send to the customer first, then sample a few cases later
Approve every case, even though only some of the flow is risky
testQuestions.explanation: Exception review matches Augment logic: AI speeds up standard cases, but risk, personal data or uncertainty goes to a human before send.

testQuestions.question: Situation: an agent completed the task, but the result was inaccurate. After the error review, you found the root cause. What should you do so the learning loop closes instead of only describing the problem?
options: Update the rule, prompt, skill or test based on the root cause, not just the incident note
Write a note and keep using the same prompt
Make the agent answer longer
Run the same request again without changes
testQuestions.explanation: The learning loop closes only when the lesson becomes a concrete update: a rule, prompt, skill or test. Logging the issue alone does not improve the next run.

slides.title: Extra: agent chain in 5 minutes
slides.subtitle: Coordinator -> specialist -> evaluator

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: After the test you can try a mini agent chain in 5 minutes without Zapier or Make: 3 separate AI chats, a clear handoff, and one quality check.

sections.heading: Do this now

sections.heading: Copyable prompt

COORDINATOR: Task: [DESCRIBE]. Split into 2 sub-tasks, assign a specialist and an evaluator. State input, output, and the handoff rule.

SPECIALIST: Input: [COORDINATOR PLAN]. Do the assigned work. Output: short summary, facts, unknowns.

EVALUATOR: Check the specialist output against criteria: facts, clarity, risk. If data is missing – return a fix list.

slides.title: Business multi-agent schema
slides.subtitle: Input → roles → evaluator → output (+ human approval)

content.whyBenefit: Understand when one AI is not enough and how to split work without coding. Practice – Coordinator + 2 specialists slide (124.5).

sections.heading: In short
sections.body: The schema shows when **one AI is not enough** and how to split work without coding. Without a clear **handoff rule**, multi-agent work becomes chaotic.

sections.heading: Business multi-agent schema
sections.body:
**1. Input** – task, data, constraints.

**2. Router** (optional) – classifies type, routes.

**3. Coordinator** – splits, assigns 2–3 specialists.

**4. Specialists** – narrow work (research, draft, calc).

**5. Evaluator** – quality check, rules, returns for fix.

**6. Output** – with a **human approval gate** before send to client or manager.

sections.heading: Handoff to another role
sections.body: Handoff – a clear rule: "When the specialist finishes X, pass to the evaluator in format Y." Without a handoff rule, multi-agent work becomes chaotic.

sections.heading: Do this now
sections.body: Fill the template below for **your process** (e.g. RFP summary or weekly report). You will reuse the same rule in practice 124.5.

sections.heading: Copyable template
sections.body: Copy and fill the [ ] fields.
sections.copyable:
Handoff rule:
From: [specialist / role]
To: [evaluator / other role]
When: when [X] is done
Format: [Y – e.g. 5 bullets + sources]
If it fails checks: return with [what to fix]
HITL before send: [yes/no – when]

sections.heading: Check
sections.body: Are "when" and "format" concrete (not vague)? Is HITL stated before send to client or manager? If not – fix before 120.55.

slides.title: Checkpoint: multi-agent schema
slides.subtitle: Agent practice path – check the schema before practice

content.title: Do you understand the multi-agent schema?
content.body: Before the quick start (124.5) and the 3A practices, quickly check that you understand the division of work. Mark done when you can name which role does what and where human approval is needed.

sections.heading: What to check
sections.body: Can you name the chain: **Input → Coordinator → 2 specialists → Evaluator → Output**? Do you know where the **human approval (HITL) gate** kicks in?

sections.heading: Mini test (1 min)
sections.body: Copy the prompt and run one coordinator + specialist pair on your topic.

slides.title: Scenario: Research agent
slides.subtitle: Search + summary + sources + golden test

scenario.narrativeLead: Unlike Module 10 theory: here you run a research agent on **your topic** and must pass an edge-case golden test (no sources).
scenario.situation: Optional scenario: research agent – search, summary, sources, plus a golden test when sources are missing.
scenario.context: Task: (1) search + summary + sources on your topic; (2) golden test – run again with an impossible query and verify “Not found” behaviour.
scenario.data: Use AI with search enabled. Fill ([X], [ROLE]). In the artefact mark a **tool trace** (did AI actually use search?).
scenario.constraints: If no sources – write “Not found: [reason]” and suggest what to fix. Do not invent links. Golden test must pass before marking done.
scenario.expectedFormat: Artefacts: (A) summary with links + tool trace; (B) golden test – “Not found” answer to an impossible query.

practicalTask.explanation: This differs from 10.5: live run + golden test. Without both parts, completion is weak.

slides.title: Practice 1: Automatize (80 %)
slides.subtitle: Form → CRM → email → Slack (platform or prompt-only)

scenario.narrativeLead: Pick a form and destinations (Sheets/CRM, email, Slack or Teams). Build the workflow and record artefacts.
scenario.situation: Automatize (80 %): rule-based flows – form to CRM, email and team notification without manual decision at each step.
scenario.context: Build a workflow: form → sheet or CRM → personalized email → Slack / Teams. Tool: Zapier/Make **or** prompt-only spec without a platform.
scenario.data: Rule-based flows. Task: build a working workflow and record artefacts – diagram, field mapping, tests, log evidence.
scenario.constraints: **Recommended:** Zapier or Make (free tier is enough). **Prompt-only fallback (no account):** describe the same flow as a 1-page spec + 2 test cases in an AI chat – without a live Zapier run. In both paths, describe error handling if the API fails. If AI logic is a separate service – note hosting (Railway / Render) and mode (always-on / cron); optional. Use the **10.64 brief** as the artefact base.
scenario.expectedFormat: Artefacts: 1-page workflow spec (trigger, input schema, conditions, actions, output, error handling), field mapping, at least 2 test cases. **Platform path:** + logs or screenshots. **Prompt-only:** + AI chat excerpt with the filled spec.

slides.title: Practice 2: Augment (15 %)
slides.subtitle: Email → AI summary → human approve → send

scenario.narrativeLead: Build a workflow with an AI summary step and a human-control rule. Record the prompt, mode and escalation.
scenario.situation: Augment (15 %): human decides, AI helps – e.g. email → summary → approval before send.
scenario.context: Flow: incoming email → AI summary (e.g. ChatGPT or Make AI module) → human approval before send → send.
scenario.data: Task: build the workflow and prepare artefacts – schema, summary prompt template, control rule (mode, condition, escalation, audit), 1–2 test cases.
scenario.constraints: Use Make or Zapier with an AI module. Clearly state who sees the summary, who approves exceptions, and what happens on reject.
scenario.expectedFormat: Artefacts: workflow schema, summary prompt, human-control rule, 1–2 test cases.

practicalTask.title: Record Augment workflow and artefacts
practicalTask.placeholder: Enter workflow summary, summary prompt and control rules…
practicalTask.template:
Summary prompt: From this email extract: (1) sender and date, (2) main topic in one sentence, (3) 3–5 bullet points.

Human-control rule: mode – exception review; condition – escalate to a human when the summary goes to a customer, includes personal data, or AI marks uncertainty; reject / escalate – stop send and return a fix list; audit – log approver, time, decision and fix.

EVALUATOR PROMPT: Check the summary: (1) tone suitable for client, (2) facts match original, (3) no excess personal data. If not – return a list of fixes.
practicalTask.explanation: Exception review lowers risk without forcing a human to approve every standard case.

slides.title: Practice 3: Autonomize (5 %)
slides.subtitle: Feedback → sentiment AI → escalation → ticket/task

scenario.narrativeLead: Build sentiment-based escalation workflow. Define thresholds and what to do in an incident.
scenario.situation: Autonomize (5 %): AI agent with limits – feedback, sentiment, escalation to ticket or task.
scenario.context: Flow (roles): **Classifier** → **Sentiment specialist** → **Escalation coordinator** → ticket. Feedback (CRM/form) → sentiment AI → escalation → ticket (Jira, Trello, Teams).
scenario.data: Task: define thresholds, escalation rules and prepare a schema. Incident plan – optional (M10 10.65).
scenario.constraints: **Recommended:** Make, n8n or Zapier with an AI module. **Prompt-only fallback:** define sentiment thresholds, escalation rules and a 1 p. schema without a platform. State when to create a ticket. Use the **10.64 brief** as the artefact base.
scenario.expectedFormat: Artefacts: workflow schema (1 p.), sentiment thresholds, escalation rules. **Optional:** link to incident plan (5 steps). **Prompt-only:** schema + thresholds are enough.

practicalTask.title: Record autonomous workflow and rules
practicalTask.placeholder: Enter sentiment thresholds, escalation rules and workflow summary…
practicalTask.template: Sentiment thresholds: if < 3 stars or negative – create ticket and notify team. Escalation rule: ticket fields “sentiment”, “original_text” (short). Incident playbook: see AUTOMATIZAVIMO_IRANKIAI_VERSLUI §20.
practicalTask.explanation: Clear rules prevent wrong mass escalation. Use the 10.64 brief as the artefact base. You can mark done without an artefact, but completion is weak – enter at least a short summary.

sections.body: Enable search or calculator on the platform and check whether AI used the tool in the answer.
sections.copyable: Use [tool – search or calculator] and provide the result with a short explanation. Task: [DESCRIBE].

sections.heading: Error handling and limits
sections.body: Add to the system prompt and try a task without data – AI must clearly say when it cannot complete the task.
sections.copyable: If the task cannot be completed: write “Failed: [reason]” and suggest what the user can fix. Never return an empty answer.

slides.title: Project summary
slides.subtitle: What next?

content.introBody: Congratulations! You finished the Agent path project. Quick start was **124.5 → 124**; required 3 practices (**121–123**) – on a platform or prompt-only. Optional 125 – extra practice.

sections.heading: Three practices and 3A
items: Automatize – routine steps without manual work
Augment – AI as helper with approval
Autonomize – limited autonomy cycles with safeguards
Workflow diagram, trigger → action → webhook – as in Module 10

sections.heading: Optional scenarios
items: Form → CRM → email, email → summary → approval
Feedback → sentiment → ticket, research / report with tools
System prompt with limits and clear “Failed” messages

sections.heading: Where to apply?
items: Automation: form, CRM, notifications
Augmentation: summaries, drafts with human approval
Autonomy: sentiment, escalation, limited tasks
Research, internal reports, integrations with Zapier / Make / n8n

content.reflectionPrompt:
**Apply:** Which artefact will I use at work within 48 hours?
**Analyze:** Which 3A band is my process in – and is security enough?
**Create:** Which one rule / prompt / test will I update after the run?

META: You are a training reflection assistant. Goal – consolidate Agent engineering project results.
INPUT: I just finished Module 12: at least one of 3 required practices (Automatize / Augment / Autonomize) and maybe optional scenarios – I have workflow diagram and artefacts.
OUTPUT: Ask 3 questions: (1) Which agent or workflow step will I apply in 24 hours? (2) What was newest? (3) What do I want to try first with a real process? After my answers, give one concrete tip.
content.nextStepCTA: Go to Module 13: Content engineering with AI
content.firstAction24h: In 24–48 hours run one workflow in your project (Zapier / Make / AI with tools) or repeat the same scenario with another form.
